claim: The operationally precise trigger for setting a humane trap for a lost dog is: confirmed evidence on trail camera that the dog is eating at a specific food station location — not before.
stance_target: Trap deployment timing protocol — camera confirmation as prerequisite
scope_conditions: Skittish/shy/survival-mode dogs; gregarious dogs may be captured by luring without a trap
quoted_support: The short and simple answer is when you have the dog eating on a trail camera in a specific location. [...] not every dog needs to be trapped. What we mean is that trapping a dog using a humane trap is safe but can be startling to a skittish dog. If you have a very friendly dog most of the time, they can be lured using calming signals instead of being trapped.

claim: A lost dog will not travel long distances to reach a trap — the trap must be placed precisely where camera evidence shows the dog is eating, not at a convenient or assumed location.
stance_target: Camera-confirmed placement as prerequisite for trap deployment effectiveness
quoted_support: The dog will not travel long distances just because you set a trap in a specific place. The location of a trap must be carefully thought out based on the behavior and where you can get the dog eating.

claim: Traps must be checked every 1-3 hours without remote camera, or monitored by cellular game camera sending real-time photos; an animal should not remain in a trap more than 30 minutes.
stance_target: Trap monitoring frequency and welfare protocol
quoted_support: Traps should be checked every 1-3 hours if you are not placing a wireless camera to watch the trap remotely. [...] you still want to be nearby if something goes into the trap. Ideally, you don't want any animal in a trap for over 30 minutes if possible.

claim: When overnight trap monitoring is not possible, the door should be tied open (bungee cord) with food refreshed so the dog can enter, eat, and build comfort with the trap — increasing likelihood of successful capture when the trap is next set.
stance_target: Trap desensitization protocol for overnight periods
scope_conditions: Overnight/unmonitored periods when trap cannot be checked frequently
quoted_support: If you cannot stay and monitor the trap overnight, for example, we recommend refreshing the smelly food in the trap and then tying the door with a bungee cord or rope. This will allow the lost pet to get some food and to see the trap is less threatening for when it is set to go off again.

claim: A captured dog must never be opened and released from the trap in the field — the entire trap must be transported to an enclosed space (garage or vehicle) before opening, to prevent escape.
stance_target: Post-capture protocol — trap transport before opening
quoted_support: We recommend taking the whole trap into a garage, enclosed area, or vehicle if the trap is small enough and remove the pet then so they cannot escape before getting them secured.

claim: Initial food station setup should use high-scent foods (canned wet dog food with gravy, deli meats, liquid smoke, barbecue sauce) placed near the escape point along with a familiar scent item (owner's dirty clothes, dog's bed).
stance_target: Food station baiting protocol — scent density and familiar anchoring
scope_conditions: Food station setup immediately after loss; applicable to all temperament types
quoted_support: Find a way to place a bowl of canned wet dog food out for them. Dog food with gravy, like ALPO, is a great example. You want to use something that has a heavy scent. Other great options are smelly deli meats, liquid smoke or barbecue sauce. It is also a good idea to place a scent item out that is familiar to the dog, like their bed or some of your dirty clothes.
